A beginner-friendly subset (common in many English-learning contexts) grouped by manner: Stops: - p b (bilabial) - t d (alveolar) - k ɡ (velar) Fricatives: - f v (labiodental) - θ ð (dental) - s z (alveolar) - ʃ ʒ (postalveolar) - h (glottal) Affricates: - tʃ dʒ Nasals: - m (bilabial) - n (alveolar) - ŋ (velar) Approximants: - j (palatal) - w (labial-velar) - ɹ (rhotic approximant, in narrower transcriptions) Lateral: - l If you learn those, you can read a large portion of dictionary IPA.
I want drills. Like, give me a list of IPA symbols and I’ll try to say what they are, then you correct me. But since this is text-only, how can we do it?

We can do it as recognition and description rather than actual audio: - You tell me the common spelling pattern or example word you associate with the symbol (without needing to mention anything about where you’re from). - Or you describe the articulation: “tongue at the teeth, no voice,” etc. - I’ll confirm and refine, and I can also give minimal pairs (pairs of words that differ by one sound) as a test. Let’s start with 10 symbols. Reply with what you think each is: 1) ʃ 2) ʒ 3) θ 4) ð 5) ŋ 6) ɪ 7) i 8) ʊ 9) u 10) ə
1 sh, 2 like zh, 3 th in think, 4 th in this, 5 ng in sing, 6 i in sit, 7 ee in see, 8 u in put, 9 oo in food, 10 uh in about.

That’s essentially correct across the board, and you mapped them to very practical cues. A couple refinements to make your mental model more “IPA-like”: - ɪ vs i: think “lax/short high front” vs “tense/long high front.” Many dictionaries use length marks too, but even without them, i is typically “more close” than ɪ. - ʊ vs u: similarly, ʊ is “near-close” and u is “close,” often with stronger rounding. Your examples (sit/see, put/food, about for schwa) are exactly how learner dictionaries teach them. Want the next set with slightly trickier ones: æ, ɛ, ʌ, ɑ, ɔ, e, o, aɪ, aʊ, ɔɪ?
Yes, but I’m already confused by ɛ vs æ vs ʌ. Do it anyway.
Totally normal. Here’s the set; answer with a word example (or just “not sure”): 1) æ 2) ɛ 3) ʌ 4) ɑ 5) ɔ 6) e (or eɪ depending on dictionary) 7) o (or oʊ depending on dictionary) 8) aɪ 9) aʊ 10) ɔɪ

1 is like a in cat, 2 is like e in bed, 3 is like u in cup, 4 is like a in father, 5 is like aw in saw, 6 is like ay in say, 7 is like oh in go, 8 is like i in time, 9 is like ow in now, 10 is like oy in boy.

Excellent. That’s the standard set of associations. To reduce the ɛ/æ/ʌ confusion, anchor them on the vowel chart: - æ is fronter and more open than ɛ (mouth more open). - ʌ is more central than both (tongue not as far front), typically with less lip activity. If you want a technical drill: we can classify each by (height, backness, rounding). For example: - æ = near-open front unrounded - ɛ = open-mid front unrounded - ʌ = open-mid central unrounded
I saw /ɜː/ and /ɝ/ and /ɚ/ in some materials. What are those?

These relate to an “r-colored” vowel category and its variants. - ɜː is a mid-central vowel (often “nurse”-type vowel in many systems) with length mark. It’s not inherently “r-colored” in the symbol itself; it’s just a central vowel. - ɝ is an r-colored (rhotic) version of a mid-central vowel: a vowel with an “r” quality as part of the vowel. - ɚ is r-colored schwa: an unstressed r-colored central vowel. In some transcription systems, instead of writing a separate r-colored vowel symbol, they write a vowel plus /r/ (or [ɹ]) depending on the analysis. So you may see different notations for similar pronunciations.
So is “rhotic” just “has an r”?

In practical terms, “rhotic” means the “r-ness” is present in the pronunciation in a way that affects the vowel or is realized as an r-like sound. In transcription, that can show up as: - a consonant /r/ or [ɹ] after a vowel, or - an r-colored vowel symbol like [ɝ] or [ɚ], or - a diacritic indicating rhoticity. The exact usage depends on the transcription tradition and what they’re trying to represent.
Another thing: sometimes I see parentheses, like /ˈ... (ə) .../. What do parentheses mean in IPA?

Parentheses in dictionary pronunciations often indicate optional sounds—segments that may be present or absent depending on context or speaking style. Example pattern: /ˈfæv(ə)rɪt/ would mean the schwa in the middle may be pronounced or may be omitted. This isn’t an IPA “phonetic” symbol per se; it’s a dictionary notation convention using IPA characters.
I also see a little raised dot or an apostrophe-looking thing for stress. You explained ˈ and ˌ. Are there other stress marks?

In IPA, the standard stress marks are: - ˈ primary - ˌ secondary Some materials might use an apostrophe-like mark ’ instead of ˈ due to typography limitations, or they might put stress marks above the line. But functionally, if you see something before a syllable that looks like a stress marker, it usually indicates that syllable is stressed.
Can you explain the difference between a phoneme and an allophone in simple terms, but still accurate?

Sure. - Phoneme: an abstract “sound category” that can change meaning if swapped with another phoneme. It’s what / / usually represents. - Allophone: a specific pronunciation variant of a phoneme that doesn’t change meaning and is often predictable by context. It’s what [ ] can represent in detail. Example idea: a language may have a phoneme /t/. That /t/ might be pronounced differently in different positions (like aspirated at the start of a stressed syllable, unreleased at the end, etc.). Those variants are allophones of /t/.
I think I get it. But then why do dictionaries give phonemic transcriptions if people don’t actually pronounce exactly that?

Because phonemic transcriptions are: - easier to learn and read, - stable across contexts (less clutter), - good for distinguishing words. Dictionaries aim to help you identify the intended pronunciation category and stress pattern. Fine phonetic details are often predictable once you know the language’s sound rules, and they can be too much information for most users.
I want to get good at reading IPA quickly. What routine should I follow?

A practical routine (15–20 minutes a day): 1) Symbol flashcards (5 minutes) - Pick 10 symbols. For each: name it, describe it (voiced/voiceless, place/manner), and give one example word. 2) Stress + syllable practice (5 minutes) - Take 5 multisyllable words in IPA. Mark primary stress, clap the syllables, then say them. 3) Minimal pairs (5 minutes) - Practice pairs that differ by one sound: /ɪ/ vs /i/, /ɛ/ vs /æ/, /s/ vs /ʃ/, /θ/ vs /s/, etc. Even if you can’t record audio, you can still test recognition: “Which one has ʃ?” “Which one has primary stress on the second syllable?” 4) Transcription reading (5 minutes) - Pick one short sentence transcribed in IPA and read it slowly, then faster, focusing on chunking. If you want, I can generate daily practice sets and you can respond with your interpretations.
Give me a sentence in IPA to practice. Something simple.

Here’s a simple one in broad IPA with common symbols: /aɪ laɪk tə lɜːn nəʊ saʊndz/ Tasks: 1) Split into words. 2) Convert each word into normal spelling (approximate). 3) Identify the stressed content words (in natural speech, even if not marked). Reply with your attempt and I’ll correct it.
I think it’s “I like to learn new sounds.”

Correct. Now, symbol notes: - aɪ = diphthong in “I” - laɪk = /l/ + /aɪ/ + /k/ - tə = reduced “to” with schwa - lɜːn = “learn” with the mid-central long vowel - nəʊ = “know/new” type vowel depending on word; here intended “new” in broad terms (some transcriptions might use /njuː/) - saʊndz = “sounds” with /aʊ/ and final /dz/ cluster If you want a slightly harder one with stress marks and a couple tricky consonants, say so.
Yes. Include ʃ, ʒ, θ, ð, and ŋ in one or two sentences if possible.

Here are two short practice sentences in broad IPA (with stress marks only where helpful): 1) /ðɪs ʃɒp sɛlz θɪn ʃiːts/ 2) /aɪ hɜːd ə ʒʌz ɪn ðə dɪˈstæns, ænd ə bɪɡ ŋɪŋ/ Tasks: - Write the intended normal spelling. - Identify each target symbol occurrence (ʃ, ʒ, θ, ð, ŋ) and say what sound it is. Note: Sentence 2 contains a playful “ŋɪŋ” sequence to force the ŋ symbol; it’s not a common real word as written. If you prefer only real words, I can rewrite it.
Sentence 1: “This shop sells thin sheets.” Sentence 2: “I heard a zhuz in the distance, and a big nging.” ð is “th” voiced, θ is “th” unvoiced, ʃ is sh, ʒ is zh, ŋ is ng.

Nice work. Sentence 1 is exactly right. Sentence 2: your decoding is correct given the intentionally odd last word. If we keep it all real-word-like, we can do: /aɪ hɜːd ə ʒʌʃ ɪn ðə dɪˈstæns, ænd ə bɪɡ bɛl ˈrɪŋɪŋ/ That would correspond roughly to “I heard a zhush in the distance, and a big bell ringing.” (The “zhush” is slangy but used.) The ŋ then appears naturally in /ˈrɪŋɪŋ/ (“ringing”). Target symbols: - ʒ in ʒʌʃ - ð in ðə - ŋ in rɪŋɪŋ
I’ve seen the symbol ɾ. What is that? And how is it different from t or d?

ɾ is an alveolar tap (or flap). It’s produced by a quick single contact of the tongue tip against the alveolar ridge. How it differs: - [t] and [d] are stops: there’s a full closure for a moment, then release. - [ɾ] is briefer and more like a rapid “tap.” It often occurs as an allophone of /t/ or /d/ in certain contexts in some languages/varieties. In transcription, you might see [ɾ] in narrow phonetic transcriptions to show that the sound is not a full [t] or [d] in that context.
I also saw ʔ. Is that like a pause?

ʔ is the glottal stop. It’s a consonant made by briefly closing the vocal folds, stopping airflow, then releasing. It can sound like a catch in the throat. It’s not exactly a pause, but it can function like a consonant that breaks the flow. In some languages it’s a regular phoneme; in others it appears as an allophone in certain environments.
Are there rules for how to syllabify IPA? Like where to put the dots?

There are conventions, but syllabification can vary depending on analysis. In dictionaries, dots are often placed to reflect a typical syllable division that helps learners. General principles often used: - Each syllable has a nucleus (usually a vowel or syllabic consonant). - Consonants between vowels are split based on permissible onset clusters (what consonants can start a syllable in that language). - Stress marks go before the stressed syllable. But you’ll see variation: some dictionaries avoid syllable dots entirely, some include them for clarity.
I want to learn to transcribe words myself into IPA. Where do I start if I’m a beginner?

Start with broad transcription and a small symbol inventory. Step-by-step: 1) Choose a reference system - Use the same dictionary consistently so you match its vowel symbols and stress conventions. 2) Start with single-syllable words - Transcribe consonants first (often easier), then vowels. 3) Add stress and syllable boundaries for multi-syllable words - Mark primary stress ˈ. - Add dots if you want. 4) Check against a dictionary - Compare your transcription to the dictionary’s and note differences. 5) Expand gradually - Add diphthongs, reduced vowels (ə), and the “special” consonants (ʃ ʒ θ ð ŋ tʃ dʒ). If you give me 10 words you want to transcribe, I can guide you: you attempt first, then I correct.
Let’s do 10 words: “strength,” “thought,” “measure,” “vision,” “queue,” “church,” “judge,” “language,” “singing,” “bathroom.”

Great set. Try your best first if you want. Meanwhile, here are broad IPA forms you might see in many learner dictionaries (exact vowels may vary by system): - strength: /streŋθ/ - thought: /θɔːt/ (sometimes /θɔt/ depending on system) - measure: /ˈmɛʒə/ (or /ˈmɛʒɚ/ in rhotic notation) - vision: /ˈvɪʒən/ - queue: /kjuː/ - church: /tʃɜːtʃ/ - judge: /dʒʌdʒ/ - language: /ˈlæŋɡwɪdʒ/ - singing: /ˈsɪŋɪŋ/ - bathroom: /ˈbɑːθ.ruːm/ (or /ˈbæθ.ruːm/ depending on system) Now, to make this a drill: pick any 5 of these and tell me what each symbol sequence indicates (stress, special consonants, vowels). For example, in /streŋθ/ identify ŋ and θ and the cluster /str/.

In strength /streŋθ/, str is a cluster, e is like short e? ŋ is ng, θ is th in think. In thought /θɔːt/ it’s th then aw long then t. In measure /ˈmɛʒə/ stress on first syllable, ɛ is like bed, ʒ is zh, ə is schwa. In queue /kjuː/ it’s k + y + oo long. In language /ˈlæŋɡwɪdʒ/ stress first, æ cat, ŋ ng, ɡ g, w, ɪ sit, dʒ j.

That’s all correct, and you’re reading it in the right “IPA mindset.” Tiny note on your “e is like short e” in /streŋθ/: in that transcription the vowel is /e/ (which some systems use), but many dictionaries would use /ɛ/ or /e/ depending on their vowel set. The important part is you correctly identified the consonant cluster and the ŋθ ending. Also, good job parsing /kjuː/ as /k/ + /j/ + /uː/ (a consonant + glide + long vowel).
I see “primary stress” but what is stress physically? Louder? Higher pitch? Longer?

Stress is a package of cues, and the exact mix depends on the language, but commonly includes: - Greater intensity (often perceived as “louder”) - Longer duration (stressed syllables can be longer) - Pitch movement (a higher pitch or noticeable pitch change) - Clearer vowel quality (unstressed vowels often reduce toward schwa) In dictionary IPA, the stress mark mainly tells you which syllable gets that prominence pattern.
Some transcriptions have a little mark for syllable length besides ː. Like half-long? Is that a thing?

Yes. IPA has: - ː = long - ˑ = half-long (sometimes written as a triangular colon-like mark) Half-long is used in more detailed phonetic transcription when a segment is longer than normal but not as long as a “long” segment.
What about tone marks? I’ve seen lines like ˥ ˩ or accents over vowels.

IPA can represent tone too, especially for tonal languages. Two common systems: 1) Tone letters (Chao tone letters): - ˥ high, ˦ mid-high, ˧ mid, ˨ mid-low, ˩ low Combinations show contours (e.g., ˥˩ falling). 2) Tone diacritics over vowels: - acute (high), grave (low), macron (mid), caron (rising), circumflex (falling), etc., depending on the system. Most general English-learner dictionaries don’t use tone marks, but you’ll see them in phonetics/linguistics materials.
I’m also confused by “linking” symbols. Sometimes there’s a little curved line between words or something like that.

In some teaching materials (not always strict IPA), you may see linking or “liaison” marks to show how words connect in continuous speech. In strict IPA, connected speech can be transcribed with spaces (word boundaries) and then phonetic detail can show assimilation, elision, etc. Some educators add extra symbols to indicate linking explicitly, but those are often pedagogical conventions rather than standard IPA. If you paste an example of the exact mark, I can identify whether it’s an IPA tie bar, a liaison mark, or a length/stress marker.
Here’s one: /neɪm‿ɪt/. There’s like a little arch between m and ɪ.

That symbol ‿ is a linking mark often used to indicate that two words are pronounced closely together as one phonetic unit (a “link”). So /neɪm‿ɪt/ indicates “name it” spoken with smooth connection between the words. It’s more common in teaching phonetics than in strict dictionary entries, but it’s useful for showing connected speech.
What about the difference between /n/ and /ŋ/? Why can’t I just write /ng/?

Because /ŋ/ is a single consonant with its own place of articulation: velar nasal. - /n/ is alveolar nasal: tongue at the alveolar ridge. - /ŋ/ is velar nasal: back of the tongue against the soft palate (velum). Writing /ng/ would imply a sequence of two consonants /n/ + /g/, which is not the same sound pattern as /ŋ/ in many contexts. Some words do have /ŋg/ sequences, but many have just /ŋ/ without a following /g/ sound. So the IPA helps distinguish: - /sɪŋ/ vs /sɪŋɡ/ (different consonant endings)
Is there a similar thing for “sh”? Like why not just write /sh/?

Exactly: /ʃ/ is one sound (a voiceless postalveolar fricative). Writing /s/ + /h/ would mean two sounds in sequence, which is different. IPA provides single symbols for single sounds even if the normal spelling uses two letters (digraphs) like sh, th, ch.
I found an IPA transcription with a tiny subscript under a consonant, like [t̪]. What is that?

[t̪] indicates a dental articulation: the tongue touches the teeth rather than the alveolar ridge. The diacritic ◌̪ (a little bridge under the symbol) marks dental. So: - [t̪] = dental t - [d̪] = dental d You might see this in detailed phonetics when describing how a sound is produced in a particular context.
Another: [ʃʲ] with a little j-like superscript. What does that mean?

The superscript ʲ indicates palatalization: the sound is produced with a secondary articulation involving raising the tongue toward the hard palate (a “y-like” quality). So [ʃʲ] is a palatalized [ʃ]. This is common in some languages as a contrastive feature, and in others it appears as a contextual phonetic detail.
I’m trying to understand “broad” vs “narrow.” Could you show the same word in broad and narrow IPA?

Sure. Here’s a generic illustrative example of how one might show increasing detail (not tied to any particular dictionary’s exact conventions): Broad (phonemic): /tɛn/ - Just the phonemes /t/ /ɛ/ /n/ Narrow (phonetic) might show: - [tʰɛ̃n] (aspiration on [t], nasalization on the vowel) or - [t̚ɛn] (unreleased [t] if it were final, etc.) A more realistic demonstration needs a specific word in a specific context (like in a sentence) because many phonetic details depend on neighboring sounds. If you give me a word and a short phrase it appears in, I can show how narrow transcription can capture predictable variations.
Okay: the phrase “ten boys.” Show broad and narrow.

Broad (phonemic): /tɛn bɔɪz/ Possible narrower (phonetic) details you might see in connected speech: - [tʰɛ̃m bɔɪz] or [tʰɛn bɔɪz] depending on whether nasal place assimilation is shown. Explanation: - The /n/ in “ten” is alveolar, but before a bilabial /b/ it may assimilate toward [m] (bilabial nasal) in fast speech: [tɛm bɔɪz]. - The [t] at the start may be aspirated [tʰ] depending on position and stress. - The vowel may show slight nasalization before the nasal: [ɛ̃]. Not every narrow transcription includes all of these; it depends on how detailed you want to be.
This is making more sense. But I still struggle to remember the vowel chart. Any memory tricks?

A few practical memory tricks: 1) Learn vowels in pairs that contrast: - i vs ɪ - u vs ʊ - e vs ɛ - ɔ vs ɒ (or ɔ vs ɑ depending on system) - ə vs ʌ 2) Use the “shape” idea: - Close vowels (i, u) feel like a smaller mouth opening. - Open vowels (æ, ɑ) feel like a larger opening. 3) Build a personal mini-chart: Write a 3x3 grid: - Front: i ɪ e ɛ æ - Central: ə ʌ ɜ - Back: u ʊ o ɔ ɑ (or ɒ) Even if the exact symbols vary, the spatial relationships will help you recall them.
Some dictionaries use /ɒ/ and some use /ɑ/. How do I not get thrown off?

Treat it like “different labels for a similar category” in that dictionary system. When you start a new dictionary: - Find its “pronunciation key” page. - Identify which symbol it uses for each common vowel category. - Make a quick mapping list for yourself: “This dictionary’s /ɒ/ corresponds to the low back rounded vowel category they use.” Also, focus on contrasts within the same system. If the dictionary uses both /ɒ/ and /ɔː/, then the key is how those two differ in that system.
Can we do a longer practice passage? Like 4–6 sentences in IPA that I can try to read.

Yes. Here’s a short passage in broad IPA with fairly common symbols and stress marks on a few multi-syllable words. It’s designed for practice, not as a perfect “one true” transcription: /ˈjɛs.tə.deɪ aɪ ˈdɪd ə ˈlɪt.əl ˈpræk.tɪs/ /aɪ lʊkt ət ə ˈdɪk.ʃə.nə.ri ænd rɛd ðə trænˈskrɪp.ʃənz/ /ət ˈfɜːst ðeɪ lʊkt ˈstreɪndʒ, bʌt ðɛn aɪ ˈnəʊ.tɪst ðə ˈpæt.ənz/ /ðə stɹɛs mɑːks hɛlpt miː ˈhɪə ðə ˈrɪð.əm/ /naʊ aɪ kæn ˈspɒt ʃ, ʒ, θ, ð, ænd ŋ ˈkwɪk.li/ /aɪ wɪl kɪp ˈpræk.tɪ.sɪŋ ˈɛv.ri deɪ/ Try to: 1) Rewrite it in normal spelling. 2) Identify at least 10 symbols and explain them.

Normal spelling: “Yesterday I did a little practice. I looked at a dictionary and read the transcriptions. At first they looked strange, but then I noticed the patterns. The stress marks helped me hear the rhythm. Now I can spot sh, zh, th, th, and ng quickly. I will keep practicing every day.” Symbols: ˈ stress, ə schwa, ʃ sh, ʒ zh, θ voiceless th, ð voiced th, ŋ ng, ɪ short i, iː long ee, ɑː long a.

That’s an accurate read-through and a good symbol inventory. A couple small notes: - In the passage, /jɛs.tə.deɪ/ includes /j/ = “y” sound. - /trænˈskrɪp.ʃənz/ shows the stress on the “-crip-” syllable and includes ʃ. - /ˈrɪð.əm/ includes ð (voiced “th”) inside the word. If you want to push your skill further, the next step is to practice reading without converting to spelling—just go from IPA to sound in your head.
I want to do that. But I still hesitate when I see clusters like /str/ or /spr/ or /θr/. Any tips?

For clusters, treat them as “onset packages” rather than individual symbols each time. Technique: 1) Drill the most common clusters as units: - /str/ /spr/ /skr/ /spl/ /skw/ /θr/ /ʃr/ (depending on your word list) 2) Practice with “nonsense syllables”: - /stra/ /stri/ /stru/ etc. - /θra/ /θri/ /θru/ 3) When reading IPA, scan for clusters and underline them mentally: Instead of reading s-t-r, read “str-” as one onset, then move to the vowel.
Can you give me a list of 30 common IPA clusters and examples, but keep it beginner-friendly?

Here are 30 common consonant clusters (written in IPA) with a simple example word for each. Quiz me. But don’t use any accent-related talk.
Got it. Here’s the quiz. For each cluster, give one example word (any you know): 1) /θr/ 2) /spl/ 3) /skw/ 4) /nd/ 5) /ŋk/ 6) /fr/ 7) /kw/ 8) /str/ 9) /mp/ 10) /skr/

Perfect. Now, to tie it back to reading IPA fluently: when you see /ˈstriːt/ you should instantly recognize /str/ + /iː/ + /t/ without sounding it out letter-by-letter.
One more confusing thing: I saw /ʍ/ in an older dictionary. What is that?

/ʍ/ is the voiceless labial-velar fricative/approximant (often described as a voiceless “w”-type sound). It’s used in some transcription traditions to represent a distinction between two “w-like” sounds in certain words. Many modern dictionaries don’t use /ʍ/ and just use /w/, but you’ll still encounter it in older materials or more detailed descriptions.
And what about /x/ and /ç/? I see them in some language textbooks.

Those are voiceless fricatives: - x = voiceless velar fricative (made at the velum; “back of the mouth” fricative) - ç = voiceless palatal fricative (made with the tongue near the hard palate; a “front” fricative) They’re common in many languages. English doesn’t use them as regular phonemes, so they feel unfamiliar at first.
I saw /ʁ/ too.

ʁ is a uvular fricative (often voiced). It’s produced with a constriction at the uvula area (further back than velar). Like x and ç, it’s common in various languages, and you’ll see it in IPA charts and language descriptions.
I’m mostly focused on reading dictionary IPA, so maybe I don’t need those yet.

Exactly. For dictionary reading, prioritize: - stress marks ˈ ˌ - common consonants (including ʃ ʒ θ ð ŋ tʃ dʒ) - common vowels and diphthongs - length ː if your dictionary uses it - schwa ə Then you can add “extra” symbols when you encounter them.
Can you give me a compact “IPA reading checklist” I can keep next to me?

Yes—here’s a compact checklist: 1) Identify stress marks: ˈ primary, ˌ secondary. 2) Split into syllables (dots if present): ... . ... 3) Scan for “special consonants”: - ʃ sh, ʒ zh, θ voiceless th, ð voiced th, ŋ ng, tʃ ch, dʒ j 4) Scan for reduced vowels: - ə schwa (often unstressed) 5) Check vowel length: - ː long 6) Recognize common diphthongs: - aɪ, aʊ, ɔɪ, eɪ, əʊ (or oʊ) 7) Blend: read syllable by syllable, giving prominence to the stressed syllable.
I want more practice with stress. Give me 20 words with IPA and I’ll mark which syllable is stressed (like 1st, 2nd, 3rd).

Here are 20 words with syllable breaks and primary stress marks. Your task: write the stressed syllable number. 1) /ˈkɒm.fə.tə.bəl/ 2) /kənˈvɪn.sɪŋ/ 3) /ˌjuː.nɪˈvɜː.sɪ.ti/ 4) /ɪkˈsplæ.nə.ʃən/ 5) /ˈdɪf.ɪ.kəl.ti/ 6) /fəˈmɪl.jə/ 7) /ˈbjuː.tɪ.fəl/ 8) /əˈtɛn.ʃən/ 9) /ˌnɛɡ.əˈtɪv.ə.ti/ 10) /ˈdɪk.ʃə.nə.ri/ 11) /prəˈnʌn.siˌeɪ.ʃən/ 12) /ˌɪn.təˈprɛt.ə.ʃən/ 13) /ˈsɪm.pəl/ 14) /əˈɡriː.mənt/ 15) /ˈkʌl.tʃə.rəl/ 16) /ˌɪn.fəˈmeɪ.ʃən/ 17) /ˈpræk.tɪs/ 18) /prəˈdʌk.tɪv/ 19) /ˈvɛr.i.ə.bəl/ 20) /əˈpɪə.rəns/ Reply like: 1) 1st, 2) 2nd, etc.

Nearly all correct. Let’s check the ones that commonly trip people up: 11) /prəˈnʌn.siˌeɪ.ʃən/ Syllables: prə | nʌn | si | eɪ | ʃən Primary stress is on nʌn (2nd syllable). Secondary stress on eɪ (4th syllable). So 11) stressed syllable = 2nd. Everything else you marked matches the primary stress mark placement: - 12) /ˌɪn.təˈprɛt.ə.ʃən/ primary stress on prɛt (3rd) correct. - 16) /ˌɪn.fəˈmeɪ.ʃən/ primary stress on meɪ (3rd) correct. Want another batch where the stress is later in the word (often harder), like “recommendation,” “identification,” etc.?
Yes. Give 20 more, and include at least 5 with secondary stress too.

Here you go. Mark the primary stressed syllable number; you can optionally note secondary stress too. 1) /rɛ.kə.mɛnˈdeɪ.ʃən/ 2) /ɪˌdɛn.tɪ.fɪˈkeɪ.ʃən/ 3) /əˌpɒs.trəˈfɪ/ 4) /ˌkɒn.səˈtreɪ.ʃən/ 5) /ˌdɪs.əˈɡriː.mənt/ 6) /ˌɪn.dɪˈvɪdʒ.u.əl/ 7) /ˌɪn.təˌrɛl.əˈteɪ.ʃən/ 8) /əˌpriː.ʃiˈeɪ.ʃən/ 9) /ˌdɛm.ənˈstreɪ.ʃən/ 10) /ˌɪm.prə.vɪˈzeɪ.ʃən/ 11) /ˌkɒm.pə.təˈbɪl.ə.ti/ 12) /ˌrɪ.prɪ.zɛnˈteɪ.ʃən/ 13) /ˌjuː.tɪ.ləˈzeɪ.ʃən/ 14) /ˌkɒn.sɪ.lɪˈeɪ.ʃən/ 15) /ˌprə.nʌn.siˈeɪ.ʃən/ 16) /ˌkɒn.trə.dɪkˈʃən/ 17) /ˌdɪ.fə.rɛn.ʃiˈeɪ.ʃən/ 18) /ˌɪn.vɛs.tɪˈɡeɪ.ʃən/ 19) /ˌɪn.təˌrʌp.ʃən/ 20) /ˌɪn.təˌræk.ʃən/ When you reply, do it like: 1) 4th, 2) 4th, etc. and if you want: “secondary on 1st/2nd…”

Let’s verify carefully by counting syllables exactly as written (each chunk between dots is one syllable). 1) /rɛ.kə.mɛnˈdeɪ.ʃən/ = rɛ(1) kə(2) mɛn(3) deɪ(4) ʃən(5) → primary on 4th. Correct. 2) /ɪˌdɛn.tɪ.fɪˈkeɪ.ʃən/ = ɪ(1) dɛn(2) tɪ(3) fɪ(4) keɪ(5) ʃən(6) → primary on 5th, not 4th. 3) /əˌpɒs.trəˈfɪ/ = ə(1) pɒs(2) trə(3) fɪ(4) → primary on 4th, not 3rd. 4) /ˌkɒn.səˈtreɪ.ʃən/ = kɒn(1) sə(2) treɪ(3) ʃən(4) → primary on 3rd. Correct. 5) /ˌdɪs.əˈɡriː.mənt/ = dɪs(1) ə(2) ɡriː(3) mənt(4) → primary on 3rd. Correct. 6) /ˌɪn.dɪˈvɪdʒ.u.əl/ = ɪn(1) dɪ(2) vɪdʒ(3) u(4) əl(5) → primary on 3rd. Correct. 7) /ˌɪn.təˌrɛl.əˈteɪ.ʃən/ = ɪn(1) tə(2) rɛl(3) ə(4) teɪ(5) ʃən(6) → primary on 5th. You wrote 5th. Correct. 8) /əˌpriː.ʃiˈeɪ.ʃən/ = ə(1) priː(2) ʃi(3) eɪ(4) ʃən(5) → primary on 4th. Correct. 9) /ˌdɛm.ənˈstreɪ.ʃən/ = dɛm(1) ən(2) streɪ(3) ʃən(4) → primary on 3rd. Correct. 10) /ˌɪm.prə.vɪˈzeɪ.ʃən/ = ɪm(1) prə(2) vɪ(3) zeɪ(4) ʃən(5) → primary on 4th. Correct. 11) /ˌkɒm.pə.təˈbɪl.ə.ti/ = kɒm(1) pə(2) tə(3) bɪl(4) ə(5) ti(6) → primary on 4th. Correct. 12) /ˌrɪ.prɪ.zɛnˈteɪ.ʃən/ = rɪ(1) prɪ(2) zɛn(3) teɪ(4) ʃən(5) → primary on 4th. Correct. 13) /ˌjuː.tɪ.ləˈzeɪ.ʃən/ = juː(1) tɪ(2) lə(3) zeɪ(4) ʃən(5) → primary on 4th. Correct. 14) /ˌkɒn.sɪ.lɪˈeɪ.ʃən/ = kɒn(1) sɪ(2) lɪ(3) eɪ(4) ʃən(5) → primary on 4th. Correct. 15) /ˌprə.nʌn.siˈeɪ.ʃən/ = prə(1) nʌn(2) si(3) eɪ(4) ʃən(5) → primary on 4th. Correct. 16) /ˌkɒn.trə.dɪkˈʃən/ = kɒn(1) trə(2) dɪk(3) ʃən(4) → primary on 4th. Correct. 17) /ˌdɪ.fə.rɛn.ʃiˈeɪ.ʃən/ = dɪ(1) fə(2) rɛn(3) ʃi(4) eɪ(5) ʃən(6) → primary on 5th. You wrote 5th. Correct. 18) /ˌɪn.vɛs.tɪˈɡeɪ.ʃən/ = ɪn(1) vɛs(2) tɪ(3) ɡeɪ(4) ʃən(5) → primary on 4th. Correct. 19) /ˌɪn.təˌrʌp.ʃən/ = ɪn(1) tə(2) rʌp(3) ʃən(4) → primary on 3rd. Correct. 20) /ˌɪn.təˌræk.ʃən/ = ɪn(1) tə(2) ræk(3) ʃən(4) → primary on 3rd. Correct. So only #2 and #3 were off, because the syllable counting is easy to misread when there are both secondary and primary stress marks.
Nice. Now I want to learn how to type IPA on my computer/phone without special software. Any tips?

Options that don’t require specialized software: 1) Copy/paste from an IPA chart - Keep a bookmarked IPA chart site. When you need a symbol (ə ʃ ŋ ˈ ː), copy and paste. 2) Use long-press on mobile (limited) - Some keyboards let you long-press letters to get variants, but IPA coverage is inconsistent. 3) Use Unicode shortcuts (platform-dependent) - Some systems allow “compose key” sequences or character viewer insertion. You can search for “Unicode character viewer” on your device and insert IPA symbols. 4) Use an online IPA keyboard - Search “IPA keyboard” in a browser. You can click symbols and copy the resulting string. If you tell me your device type (just “Windows/Mac/iOS/Android”), I can suggest the easiest built-in method.
Let’s keep it general. Can you give me the Unicode names for the most common ones so I can search them?

Sure—here are common IPA symbols with their Unicode names (you can paste the name into a character search tool on many systems): - ə = LATIN SMALL LETTER SCHWA - ʃ = LATIN SMALL LETTER ESH - ʒ = LATIN SMALL LETTER EZH - ŋ = LATIN SMALL LETTER ENG - θ = GREEK SMALL LETTER THETA - ð = LATIN SMALL LETTER ETH - ɪ = LATIN LETTER SMALL CAPITAL I - ʊ = LATIN SMALL LETTER UPSILON - ɑ = LATIN SMALL LETTER ALPHA - ɔ = LATIN SMALL LETTER OPEN O - æ = LATIN SMALL LETTER AE - ɛ = LATIN SMALL LETTER OPEN E - ʌ = LATIN SMALL LETTER TURNED V - ɜ = LATIN SMALL LETTER REVERSED OPEN E - ɡ = LATIN SMALL LETTER SCRIPT G - ɹ = LATIN SMALL LETTER TURNED R - ˈ = MODIFIER LETTER VERTICAL LINE (primary stress) - ˌ = MODIFIER LETTER LOW VERTICAL LINE (secondary stress) - ː = MODIFIER LETTER TRIANGULAR COLON (length mark) - ʔ = LATIN LETTER GLOTTAL STOP
That list is super helpful. Another question: why does IPA sometimes use Greek letters like θ?

IPA is based on the Latin alphabet but borrows from Greek and other sources when needed to represent distinct sounds clearly. Greek letters like θ are visually distinctive and historically used in phonetic transcription traditions, so they became part of the IPA inventory.
Is IPA the best system for pronunciation, or are there alternatives?

IPA is the most widely used standardized system in linguistics and many dictionaries, but alternatives exist: - Dictionary-specific respelling systems (like “shuh-WAH” style) that are easier for casual readers but less precise. - Other phonetic alphabets (like Americanist phonetic notation in some contexts). - For some languages, romanization systems with diacritics. For learning to interpret dictionary pronunciations across many resources, IPA is generally the most transferable.
I’m going to paste a random IPA entry I saw: /ˌsɪm.pəˈθɛt.ɪk/. Can you walk me through it?

Yes. /ˌsɪm.pəˈθɛt.ɪk/ - ˌ = secondary stress on the first syllable: sɪm - sɪm = /s/ + /ɪ/ + /m/ - . = syllable boundary - pə = /p/ + /ə/ (schwa) - ˈ = primary stress on the next syllable: θɛt - θɛt = /θ/ (voiceless th) + /ɛ/ + /t/ - . = syllable boundary - ɪk = /ɪ/ + /k/ So it’s 4 syllables: sɪm | pə | θɛt | ɪk, with primary stress on θɛt.
Great. Now do /ˌkær.ɪk.təˈrɪs.tɪk/.

/ˌkær.ɪk.təˈrɪs.tɪk/ Syllables: kær | ɪk | tə | rɪs | tɪk - ˌ secondary stress on kær - kær: /k/ + /æ/ + /r/ (r symbol depends on system; indicates an r sound) - ɪk: /ɪ/ + /k/ - tə: /t/ + /ə/ - ˈ primary stress on rɪs - rɪs: r + ɪ + s - tɪk: t + ɪ + k So: 5 syllables, primary stress on the 4th syllable (rɪs).
I want to understand syllable structure terms: onset, nucleus, coda. Explain with IPA examples.

In a syllable: - Onset: consonant(s) before the vowel - Nucleus: the central element, usually a vowel (or syllabic consonant) - Coda: consonant(s) after the nucleus Example with /strɛŋθ/ (strength): - One syllable. - Onset: /str/ - Nucleus: /ɛ/ (or whichever vowel symbol the system uses) - Coda: /ŋθ/ Example with /ˈpæ.tən/ (pattern, simplified): - First syllable /pæ/: - Onset /p/ - Nucleus /æ/ - Coda none - Second syllable /tən/: - Onset /t/ - Nucleus /ə/ - Coda /n/ These terms help describe why syllable boundaries go where they do.
Are syllable boundaries in IPA always objective?

Not always. There are clear cases, but many cases depend on analysis choices: - Some consonants can be ambisyllabic (belonging to both syllables) in some analyses. - Different syllabification rules can apply depending on whether you prioritize phonotactics, morphology, or phonetic timing. So syllable dots in dictionaries are helpful guides, but not always the only “correct” division.
I’m curious about the symbol ɾ again. Is it ever written in dictionaries?

Some learner dictionaries stick to phonemic symbols and won’t use ɾ. But more phonetically oriented dictionaries, pronunciation guides, or linguistics resources sometimes use ɾ to show a tap/flap realization in certain contexts. So: uncommon in mainstream dictionaries, common in phonetics materials.
I want to learn IPA for other languages too. Does the same symbol always mean the same sound?

The intended articulatory target of each IPA symbol is consistent: e.g., [m] is a bilabial nasal, [i] is a close front unrounded vowel, [ʃ] is a voiceless postalveolar fricative. However, when people transcribe a language phonemically, they may choose symbols that represent phoneme categories rather than exact phonetic realizations. So the symbol’s “core meaning” is stable, but the exact phonetic details can vary slightly across languages and contexts.
Can you give me a small IPA “starter pack” for reading, like 50 symbols max, with descriptions?

Here’s a compact starter pack (not all of IPA, just the most common for many dictionary transcriptions), grouped: Stress/length: - ˈ primary stress - ˌ secondary stress - ː long Consonants (basic): - p b t d k ɡ - f v s z h - m n ŋ - l r (or ɹ) - w j Consonants (common “special”): - θ ð - ʃ ʒ - tʃ dʒ - ʔ (sometimes) Vowels (common set): - i ɪ e ɛ æ - ə ʌ ɜ - u ʊ o ɔ ɑ (or ɒ depending on system) Diphthongs (common): - eɪ aɪ ɔɪ - aʊ - əʊ (or oʊ) That’s around 40-ish items depending on which vowel symbols your dictionary uses. With these, you can read a large fraction of entries.
This has been a lot. Can you give me a structured plan for the next 4 weeks to get comfortable with IPA?

Week 1: Core consonants + stress - Learn: ʃ ʒ θ ð ŋ tʃ dʒ, plus ˈ ˌ and syllable dots. - Daily: 10-minute symbol drill + 10 words from the dictionary (read IPA, then check spelling). Week 2: Core vowels - Learn: i ɪ e ɛ æ ə ʌ u ʊ ɔ ɑ (or ɒ). - Daily: minimal pair recognition (i/ɪ, u/ʊ, ɛ/æ, ə/ʌ) + read 10 IPA entries. Week 3: Diphthongs + length - Learn: aɪ aʊ ɔɪ eɪ əʊ and ː. - Daily: read 5 multi-syllable words + 2 short sentences in IPA; mark stress. Week 4: Speed + self-transcription - Daily: choose 15 words, try to transcribe them broadly, then compare to dictionary IPA. - Add: a few narrow symbols as curiosity (ʔ, ɾ, aspiration ʰ) but keep your main focus on broad reading. If you want, you can report back at the end of each week with what symbols still slow you down, and we can adjust the plan.

I keep mixing up indicative and subjunctive in Spanish. Is there a simple rule for when to use the subjunctive?

There isn’t one single “simple rule” that works 100% of the time, but there is a practical way to think about it that clears up most cases: - Indicative = you’re presenting something as a fact, certain, observed, or asserted as true (in your mind or in the speaker’s frame). - Subjunctive = you’re not asserting it as a fact; it’s filtered through doubt, desire, emotion, evaluation, possibility, denial, or it’s not yet realized (often hypothetical or “pending”). A very usable shortcut: if the main clause expresses a reaction/attitude (I want, I hope, I doubt, I’m happy, it’s important…) toward another clause, the second clause often goes subjunctive. Example: - Sé que viene. (I know he’s coming → asserted as fact) - Dudo que venga. (I doubt he’s coming → not asserted as fact) - Quiero que venga. (I want him to come → not factual, desired) If you tell me a few sentences you’ve gotten wrong (or ones you hesitate on), I’ll explain exactly why each one is indicative vs subjunctive.

That “fact vs not fact” thing sounds good, but then I see “No creo que…” and sometimes it’s subjunctive, and then “Creo que…” is indicative. But I also saw “No creo que es…” somewhere and I got confused. Why can’t it just be consistent?

It actually is consistent in standard Spanish; the inconsistency is usually from (1) nonstandard usage, (2) context changing what’s being asserted, or (3) people simplifying incorrectly. Core pattern: - Creo que + indicative: Creo que es verdad. / Creo que viene mañana. - No creo que + subjunctive: No creo que sea verdad. / No creo que venga mañana. Why? Because “creo que…” asserts the embedded proposition as likely true in the speaker’s view; “no creo que…” denies belief, so the proposition is not asserted as true → subjunctive. So “No creo que es…” is generally considered incorrect in careful standard usage. You might see it in some informal speech, in certain regions, or from learners. But the norm taught is “No creo que sea…” There’s one nuance: if “no creo” is not really “I don’t believe,” but more like “I don’t think (it matters/it’s relevant)” or if you’re contradicting someone and actually asserting the opposite, you might restructure the sentence: - No creo que sea verdad; creo que es mentira. (subjunctive then indicative) But you wouldn’t normally put indicative after “no creo que” in standard Spanish. If you want consistency, you can memorize a few big triggers: - doubt/denial: dudar, no creer, negar, no es cierto que → subjunctive - desire/command: querer, pedir, recomendar, insistir en que → subjunctive - emotion: alegrarse de que, temer que, molestar que → subjunctive - impersonal evaluations: es importante que, es necesario que → subjunctive

Okay but then “es cierto que” is indicative, right? But “no es cierto que” is subjunctive. That part I sort of get. But then I saw “es posible que” uses subjunctive even though it sounds like it could be true. It’s possible, so why not indicative?

Because “possible” isn’t the same as “asserted as true.” “Es posible que…” explicitly marks the proposition as uncertain. Spanish treats that uncertainty as non-assertive, so it triggers subjunctive: - Es posible que venga. (It’s possible he’ll come.) - Es probable que venga. (It’s probable he’ll come.) - Puede que venga. (He may come.) → also subjunctive Indicative is for when you present it as true/real in your discourse: - Es cierto que viene. (It’s certain/true that he’s coming.) - Es evidente que viene. (It’s evident that he’s coming.) - Está claro que viene. (It’s clear that he’s coming.) So the dividing line isn’t “could be true” vs “not true.” It’s “asserted as true” vs “not asserted as true.” A handy gradient: - certainty → indicative (es cierto/evidente/seguro que…) - probability/possibility → subjunctive (es posible/probable que…, puede que…) - denial/doubt → subjunctive (no es cierto que…, dudo que…)

I guess. But then “pienso que” is indicative, but “no pienso que” should be subjunctive? It feels weird to switch just because of “no.” Also sometimes I want to say “I don’t think he comes” like describing my opinion, not doubting reality. I’m stuck.

You’re running into a real issue: English “I don’t think…” often functions as a softener (“in my opinion…”) rather than literal doubt. Spanish tends to treat “no pienso/creo que…” as actual lack of belief, which leads to subjunctive. Default patterns: - Pienso que viene. (indicative) - No pienso que venga. (subjunctive) = I don’t think he’s coming. But Spanish speakers frequently avoid “No pienso que…” and instead say: - Pienso que no viene. (indicative) = I think he isn’t coming. This is very common because it keeps the indicative and makes the negation belong clearly to the embedded proposition. So if you mean “My view is: he is not coming,” Spanish often prefers: - Creo que no viene. / Pienso que no viene. If you mean “I’m not convinced that he’s coming” (lack of belief), then: - No creo que venga. Both can be translated as “I don’t think he’s coming” in English, which is exactly why it’s confusing. Try this English-to-Spanish mapping: 1) “I think not-X” (asserting not-X) → Creo que no + indicative. 2) “I don’t think X” (not asserting X) → No creo que + subjunctive.
That actually helps. But then there’s “aunque.” Sometimes it’s subjunctive, sometimes indicative. That blows up the whole “fact vs not fact” idea.

“Aunque” is one of the classic places where the “asserted vs non-asserted” idea is exactly what’s happening, but you have to be precise: it depends on whether the speaker is presenting the “aunque” clause as known/real, or as hypothetical/unknown. 1) Aunque + indicative = the speaker treats it as a fact (concession about a real situation): - Aunque llueve, voy a salir. (Even though it’s raining, I’m going out.) → It is raining. 2) Aunque + subjunctive = the speaker treats it as hypothetical, uncertain, or not yet verified: - Aunque llueva, voy a salir. (Even if it rains, I’m going out.) → It may rain; not sure. So “aunque” doesn’t “break the rule.” It shows the rule: indicative for conceded facts; subjunctive for conceded possibilities. You can test it with “even though” vs “even if”: - even though (fact) → aunque + indicative - even if (hypothetical) → aunque + subjunctive
Fine. What about “cuando”? I learned “cuando + future” uses subjunctive, like “cuando tenga tiempo,” but that seems arbitrary. Why can’t I say “cuando tengo tiempo, lo haré”?

Because Spanish doesn’t use the present indicative in a subordinate time clause to refer to a future event in the same way English does. Spanish marks future reference in certain subordinate clauses with the subjunctive. Rule of thumb for time clauses introduced by: cuando, en cuanto, tan pronto como, hasta que, después de que, antes de que (and a few others): - If the action is habitual/general (repeated, timeless) or refers to past/present reality → indicative. - If it refers to a future event that hasn’t happened yet → subjunctive. Examples: Habitual/general (indicative): - Cuando tengo tiempo, leo. (Whenever I have time, I read.) → general habit. - Cuando llegas tarde, me llamas. (When you arrive late, you call me.) → habitual. Future (subjunctive): - Cuando tenga tiempo, leeré. (When I have time (in the future), I will read.) - Te llamaré cuando llegues. (I’ll call you when you arrive.) So “cuando tengo tiempo, lo haré” clashes: “tengo” reads as habitual or present, but “lo haré” is future. Spanish wants “cuando tenga tiempo, lo haré.”
But I’ve heard “cuando llegas, me llamas” used to talk about a future plan, like instructions. That seems like future but it’s indicative.

Good catch. Present indicative can sometimes be used with future meaning in Spanish, especially for scheduled events, instructions, or vivid “present” narration. But with “cuando” clauses, the standard distinction still applies most of the time. There are two different things you might be hearing: 1) Habitual/instructional present (sounds like a routine): - Cuando llegas, me llamas. (When you arrive, you call me.) → like giving step-by-step instructions or describing what the person is supposed to do as a routine. It frames it as a “script,” not as a single uncertain future event. 2) Single future event (subjunctive is preferred): - Cuando llegues, llámame. (When you arrive, call me.) - Cuando llegues, me llamas. (Some speakers may say this, but “llegues” is the clear standard for that “as soon as you arrive (in the future)” meaning.) If you want to be safe and standard: use subjunctive in “cuando” time clauses when it’s a future, not-yet-realized event.

Okay. Another one: “Si.” I learned “si + present” uses indicative, but then there’s “si tuviera” and “si hubiera.” Why isn’t that subjunctive too? Or is it? It looks like subjunctive but it’s after “si.”

It is subjunctive forms, but Spanish treats “si” conditionals as a special system with their own patterns. The mood in the “si” clause depends on the type of condition: 1) Real/possible condition (likely or open): - Si tengo tiempo, voy. (If I have time, I’ll go.) → present indicative. - Si tienes hambre, come. (If you’re hungry, eat.) 2) Hypothetical/unlikely or contrary-to-fact present/future: - Si tuviera tiempo, iría. (If I had time, I would go.) → imperfect subjunctive in the si-clause + conditional in main clause. 3) Counterfactual past: - Si hubiera/hubiese tenido tiempo, habría ido. (If I had had time, I would have gone.) → pluperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect. So yes, it’s subjunctive morphology, but the “si” clause has fixed pairings: - Si + present indicative → (present/future/imperative main clause) - Si + imperfect subjunctive → conditional - Si + pluperfect subjunctive → conditional perfect One thing: standard Spanish avoids “Si tendría…” (conditional in the si-clause). If you see “Si tendría tiempo…” that’s nonstandard (though it appears in some regions). Standard is “Si tuviera tiempo…”
This is exactly why I’m frustrated. It’s like a million triggers and exceptions. I want something systematic. Like, can you categorize all the main uses of subjunctive and give me a checklist?

Yes. A systematic checklist that covers most of what you’ll encounter: A) Subjunctive in noun clauses (introduced by “que” most often) Use subjunctive when the main clause expresses: 1) Volition / desire / preference: querer que, preferir que, esperar que (often), desear que - Quiero que vengas. 2) Commands / influence: pedir que, recomendar que, insistir en que, mandar que, prohibir que - Te recomiendo que estudies. 3) Emotion / reaction: alegrarse de que, sentir que (as “to regret”), temer que, molestar que, sorprender que - Me alegra que estés aquí. 4) Doubt / denial / uncertainty: dudar que, no creer que, negar que, no es cierto que, es posible que - Dudo que tenga razón. 5) Impersonal value judgments / necessity: es importante que, es necesario que, es mejor que, conviene que, hace falta que - Es necesario que lo hagas. Use indicative in noun clauses when the main clause: 6) States knowledge / perception / assertion: saber que, ver que, oír que, es cierto que, está claro que, creer que (affirmative), pensar que (affirmative) - Sé que viene. / Creo que viene. B) Subjunctive in adjective (relative) clauses (who/that/which) Use subjunctive when the antecedent is non-specific, unknown, hypothetical, or negated: - Busco un libro que sea fácil. (any book that fits; not a specific one) - No hay nadie que lo sepa. (there isn’t anyone who knows it) Indicative when the antecedent is specific/known: - Busco el libro que es fácil. (the one that is easy; specific) C) Subjunctive in adverbial clauses (time, purpose, condition, concession) 1) Time (future not realized): cuando, en cuanto, tan pronto como, hasta que, después de que - Te llamaré cuando llegue. 2) Purpose: para que, a fin de que - Lo hago para que estés tranquilo. 3) Condition: con tal de que, a menos que, en caso de que - Iré con tal de que me avises. 4) Concession/hypothesis: aunque (hypothetical), como (in threats “como no…”), etc. - Aunque sea difícil, lo intentaré. If you want, we can turn that into an actual decision tree and practice with targeted minimal pairs.
Let’s do minimal pairs because I keep thinking I understand and then I mess it up. Start with noun clauses. Give me pairs where changing one word flips the mood.

Great. Here are some minimal pairs; I’ll give you both versions and the key meaning difference. 1) Es cierto que… (indicative) vs No es cierto que… (subjunctive) - Es cierto que tienes razón. (It’s true you’re right.) - No es cierto que tengas razón. (It’s not true you’re right.) 2) Creo que… (indicative) vs No creo que… (subjunctive) - Creo que viene hoy. (I think he’s coming today.) - No creo que venga hoy. (I don’t think he’s coming today.) 3) Me parece que… (indicative) vs No me parece que… (subjunctive) - Me parece que está bien. (It seems to me it’s fine.) - No me parece que esté bien. (It doesn’t seem to me it’s fine.) 4) Es evidente que… (indicative) vs Es posible que… (subjunctive) - Es evidente que miente. (It’s obvious he’s lying.) - Es posible que mienta. (It’s possible he’s lying.) 5) Está claro que… (indicative) vs No está claro que… (subjunctive) - Está claro que lo sabe. (It’s clear he knows it.) - No está claro que lo sepa. (It’s not clear he knows it.) 6) Me alegra que… (subjunctive) vs Me alegra saber que… (indicative inside after “saber”) - Me alegra que estés aquí. (I’m happy you’re here.) - Me alegra saber que estás aquí. (I’m happy to know you’re here.) Try rewriting these in your own words with a different verb (e.g., “hacer,” “poder,” “tener”) and I’ll correct them.

Okay: 1) Es cierto que él puede venir. 2) No es cierto que él puede venir. 3) Creo que él tenga tiempo. 4) No creo que él tiene tiempo. 5) Me alegra que él está aquí. 6) Me alegra saber que él esté aquí.

Good—this shows exactly the typical flips. 1) Es cierto que él puede venir. ✅ (indicative “puede” is correct) More natural without “él” unless contrast: Es cierto que puede venir. 2) No es cierto que él puede venir. ❌ → No es cierto que (subjunctive) Correct: No es cierto que (él) pueda venir. 3) Creo que él tenga tiempo. ❌ → “Creo que” (affirmative) usually takes indicative. Correct: Creo que (él) tiene tiempo. 4) No creo que él tiene tiempo. ❌ → “No creo que” takes subjunctive. Correct: No creo que (él) tenga tiempo. Or common alternative: Creo que (él) no tiene tiempo. (indicative) 5) Me alegra que él está aquí. ❌ → emotion → subjunctive Correct: Me alegra que (él) esté aquí. 6) Me alegra saber que él esté aquí. ❌ → “saber que” is factual → indicative Correct: Me alegra saber que (él) está aquí. If you want a “debugging” method: identify the main verb (es cierto / no es cierto / creo / no creo / me alegra / me alegra saber) and label it as FACT-ASSERTING vs ATTITUDE/UNCERTAINTY. Then pick the mood.
I always forget which verbs are “fact asserting.” Like “me parece que” feels like an opinion, not a fact. Why is it indicative?

Because “me parece que” is typically used to present your assessment as a statement about reality (“it seems to me that…”), not as a desire/doubt. It’s closer to “I think” than to “I doubt.” - Me parece que está cansado. (I think/seems he is tired.) → you’re asserting “he is tired” as your conclusion. But negation changes it: - No me parece que esté cansado. (It doesn’t seem to me that he’s tired.) → you are not asserting “he is tired”; you’re rejecting that proposition → subjunctive. Also, there are cases where “me parece bien/mal que…” is different: - Me parece bien que vengas. (I think it’s good that you come.) → evaluation/judgment → subjunctive. So: - me parece que + indicative (perception/assessment) - me parece bien/mal que + subjunctive (value judgment)
Ugh. So the same phrase “me parece” can do both. How am I supposed to catch that in real time?

Look at what comes immediately after: - me parece QUE + clause → usually indicative (it seems that…) - me parece BIEN/MAL/RARE/IMPORTANTE, etc. QUE + clause → subjunctive (it seems good/bad/important that…) Compare: - Me parece que no entiendes. (I think you don’t understand.) - Me parece mal que no entiendas. (I think it’s bad that you don’t understand.) → judgment That “adjective/adverb of evaluation” is the giveaway.
Okay, adjective clauses then. I always mess up “Busco un profesor que…” I never know if it’s subjunctive.

Use this test: Do you have a specific professor in mind (known/existing), or are you describing an unknown one you hope to find? - Specific/known → indicative: - Busco al profesor que me enseñó el año pasado. (I’m looking for the professor who taught me last year.) → he exists and is identified. - Quiero hablar con la profesora que sabe mucho de gramática. (I want to talk to the professor who knows a lot…) → specific professor. - Non-specific/desired/unknown → subjunctive: - Busco un profesor que explique bien el subjuntivo. (I’m looking for a professor who explains the subjunctive well.) → any such professor, not identified. - Necesito una persona que me ayude. (I need someone to help me.) → not a specific person. Negation almost always pushes subjunctive: - No hay nadie que lo sepa. (There isn’t anyone who knows it.) - No conozco a nadie que hable japonés. (I don’t know anyone who speaks Japanese.) → subjunctive because “anyone” is not asserted to exist in your known set. But if you do know such a person, you’d say: - Conozco a alguien que habla japonés. (indicative)
Wait, “No conozco a nadie que habla…” sounds plausible to me because I’m stating a fact about my social circle. Why subjunctive?

Because the relative clause is describing an entity (“nadie”) that is explicitly non-existent in your knowledge set. You’re not asserting the existence of “someone who speaks Japanese” within what you know; you’re denying it. Spanish treats that as non-assertive → subjunctive. Think of it as: the relative clause is inside the scope of negation. You’re not saying “there exists a person who speaks Japanese”; you’re saying “there does not exist (for me) such a person.” So: - No conozco a nadie que hable japonés. ✅ If you used indicative there, it would sound like you’re talking about a specific person you know and then contradicting yourself. The subjunctive matches the logical structure: “anyone who might speak Japanese (if such a person existed in my circle).”
Then what about “Busco un libro que tiene fotos”? I might not know which book, but I’m pretty sure such books exist. Is that still subjunctive?

Usually yes, still subjunctive, because the key is whether you’re referring to a specific book, not whether such books exist in the world. - Busco un libro que tenga fotos. (I’m looking for a book that has photos.) → non-specific; requirement. - Busco un libro que tiene fotos. (indicative) tends to imply you have a particular book in mind (maybe you saw it earlier) and you’re identifying it: “I’m looking for a book—the one that has photos.” If you want to be explicit: - Busco un libro que tenga fotos, cualquiera. (any one) - Busco el libro que tiene fotos. (the one that has photos)
Purpose clauses: “para que.” Always subjunctive, right? Are there any exceptions?

“Para que” introducing a clause with a subject (explicit or implied) virtually always takes subjunctive: - Lo hice para que me entendieras. - Te lo explico para que lo sepas. - Habla más despacio para que podamos seguirte. If the subject is the same and you can use an infinitive, Spanish often prefers infinitive instead of “para que”: - Lo hice para entenderlo. (same subject) - Estudio para aprender. (same subject) So the “exception” is not indicative; it’s switching structure to infinitive.
Time clauses again. “Después de que” sometimes I see indicative, sometimes subjunctive. Which is it?

This is one of the messy ones because usage varies and people disagree, but there’s a strong standard guideline: - Después de que + subjunctive when the action is pending/future relative to the main clause: - Te llamaré después de que llegues. (I’ll call you after you arrive.) - Después de que + indicative when it refers to a completed, known event (often past): - Te llamé después de que llegaste. (I called you after you arrived.) Similarly: - Antes de que + subjunctive almost always, because “before” points to something not yet realized at the time of the main action: - Salí antes de que llegaran. For “después de que,” many speakers use subjunctive even with past reference in some regions, but if you stick to the “known completed event = indicative” rule, you’ll be understood and it aligns with many grammar references.
You keep saying “many speakers” and “regions.” That’s not helpful when I’m trying to be correct. I want a standard to follow.

Fair. If your goal is a consistent, broadly accepted standard for writing and careful speech: - Use indicative after “después de que” when the subordinate action is presented as completed/known (especially in past narration). - Use subjunctive after “después de que” when the subordinate action is future/pending. So: - Ayer lo hice después de que terminé. (indicative) - Mañana lo haré después de que termine. (subjunctive) That’s a clean standard you can apply.
Give me a bunch of tricky sentences and make me choose. I’ll answer and you correct. I need repetition.

Choose indicative or subjunctive (and conjugate the verb in parentheses). I’ll mix categories. 1) No creo que ella (tener) razón. 2) Es obvio que ellos (estar) cansados. 3) Busco a alguien que (saber) explicarlo bien. 4) Conozco a alguien que (saber) explicarlo bien. 5) Te lo diré cuando (poder) hacerlo. 6) Cuando (ser) niño, jugaba mucho. 7) Aunque (llover), vamos a salir. (We can see it’s raining now.) 8) Aunque (llover), vamos a salir. (Even if it rains.) 9) No hay nada que me (molestar) más que eso. 10) Me gusta que tú me (decir) la verdad. 11) Pienso que no (venir) hoy. 12) No pienso que (venir) hoy. 13) Es posible que (llegar) tarde. 14) Es cierto que (llegar) tarde. 15) Lo hago para que tú (entender) mejor. Answer with just the conjugated forms (e.g., “1) tenga…”) and we’ll go through.
1) tiene 2) están 3) sabe 4) sepa 5) puedo 6) era 7) llueve 8) llueva 9) molesta 10) dices 11) viene 12) viene 13) llega 14) llegue 15) entiendes

Corrections: 1) No creo que ella (tener) razón. → tenga ✅ subjunctive You wrote “tiene” (indicative) but “no creo que” triggers subjunctive. 2) Es obvio que ellos (estar) cansados. → están ✅ indicative (good) 3) Busco a alguien que (saber) explicarlo bien. → sepa ✅ subjunctive You wrote “sabe.” Non-specific “alguien” you’re looking for → subjunctive. 4) Conozco a alguien que (saber) explicarlo bien. → sabe ✅ indicative You flipped it. “Conozco a alguien…” asserts existence. 5) Te lo diré cuando (poder) hacerlo. → pueda ✅ subjunctive (future time clause) You wrote “puedo.” 6) Cuando (ser) niño, jugaba mucho. → era ✅ indicative (past habitual) (good) 7) Aunque (llover), vamos a salir. (It’s raining now.) → llueve ✅ indicative (good) 8) Aunque (llover), vamos a salir. (Even if it rains.) → llueva ✅ subjunctive (good) 9) No hay nada que me (molestar) más que eso. → moleste ✅ subjunctive You wrote “molesta.” “No hay nada que…” → non-existent antecedent. 10) Me gusta que tú me (decir) la verdad. → digas ✅ subjunctive You wrote “dices.” Also note: “decir” is irregular: diga/digas/diga… 11) Pienso que no (venir) hoy. → viene ✅ indicative (good) 12) No pienso que (venir) hoy. → venga ✅ subjunctive You wrote “viene.” 13) Es posible que (llegar) tarde. → llegue ✅ subjunctive You wrote “llega.” 14) Es cierto que (llegar) tarde. → llega ✅ indicative You wrote “llegue.” 15) Lo hago para que tú (entender) mejor. → entiendas ✅ subjunctive You wrote “entiendes.” Pattern-wise, most of your “errors” are exactly: you default to indicative in subordinate clauses. The fix is to force yourself to identify the trigger category first.
I hate that I also have to remember irregular subjunctive forms while I’m trying to decide mood. Like “diga,” “entienda,” “pueda.” My brain just freezes.

That’s completely normal: decision (mood) + morphology (forms) at the same time is heavy. Two strategies: 1) Separate the tasks in practice: - Step A: decide mood only (don’t conjugate; just label “IND” or “SUBJ”). - Step B: conjugate in that mood. 2) Build the “subjunctive irregular survival kit” (high frequency): Present subjunctive stems: - tener → tenga - venir → venga - decir → diga - hacer → haga - poner → ponga - salir → salga - traer → traiga - saber → sepa - poder → pueda - querer → quiera - dar → dé - ir → vaya - ser → sea - estar → esté - haber → haya If you can instantly recall those, a huge portion of subjunctive usage becomes less stressful.
I also don’t get “esperar que.” Sometimes I see indicative after it, sometimes subjunctive. Like “Espero que estás bien” vs “Espero que estés bien.” Which is right?

In standard grammar, “esperar que” expressing hope/expectation about something not confirmed generally takes subjunctive: - Espero que estés bien. (I hope you’re well.) → you’re not asserting as fact; it’s a wish. Why do you see “Espero que estás bien”? - In some contexts, speakers use indicative when “espero” is closer to “I assume / I expect” and they’re treating it as basically true (or as a polite formula). It’s common in casual speech in some places, but “Espero que estés bien” is the safer standard. You can keep it consistent like this: - Hope/wish → espero que + subjunctive: Espero que venga. Espero que estés bien. - Wait for → “esperar a que” + subjunctive (because it’s a future event): Espero a que llegues. - Expect/assume (less common with “que,” but possible) → can drift to indicative, but you can avoid it by using “supongo que” / “imagino que” / “me parece que” if you mean assumption.
“Espero a que llegues” is weird. Why not infinitive? Like “espero llegar” or “espero que llegue” I’m mixing them up.

There are three different structures: 1) esperar + infinitive (same subject): - Espero llegar temprano. (I hope to arrive early / I expect to arrive early.) Subject of “espero” = subject of “llegar” (I). 2) esperar que + subjunctive (different subject, or you’re expressing hope about someone/something else): - Espero que llegues temprano. (I hope you arrive early.) Subject of “espero” = I; subject of “llegues” = you. 3) esperar a que + subjunctive (wait for something to happen): - Espero a que llegues. (I’m waiting for you to arrive.) This is “wait,” not “hope.” So if you mean “wait,” include “a”: esperar a que. If you mean “hope,” it’s esperar que. If it’s same subject, infinitive is often simplest.
Great, another split. Also: “temer que” and “tener miedo de que.” Are those always subjunctive?

Yes, typically they trigger subjunctive because they express fear (emotion) about a potential situation: - Temo que sea tarde. - Tengo miedo de que se enoje. - Me da miedo que no funcione. But there’s a nuance with “temer” that can mean “suspect/believe” in older or formal usage (“me temo que…”): - Me temo que tienes razón. (I’m afraid you’re right.) Here many speakers use indicative because the speaker is basically acknowledging it as true (resigned conclusion). You’ll also hear subjunctive, but indicative is very common in that fixed phrase. So: - fear as possibility → subjunctive - “me temo que…” as “I’m afraid (it’s true)” → often indicative
That’s exactly the kind of thing that makes me feel like I’ll never get it. Can we do a decision tree that works even with these nuances?

A workable decision tree for noun clauses: Step 1: What does the main clause do to the embedded proposition? A) Asserts it as true/known/perceived → IND B) Does not assert it; expresses attitude/need/uncertainty/denial → SUBJ Step 2: If it’s one of the “borderline verbs,” ask: - Is the speaker presenting it as a conclusion about reality (IND)? - Or as a wish/command/doubt (SUBJ)? Borderline/common toggles: - esperar que: usually SUBJ (hope), occasionally IND (assume) - me temo que: often IND (resigned belief), can be SUBJ (fear) - parecer: “me parece que” IND; “me parece bien/mal que” SUBJ - pensar/creer: affirmative IND; negated SUBJ; but “creo que no…” is IND (asserting negation) - puede que / es posible que: SUBJ - igual / quizá(s) / tal vez: can be IND or SUBJ depending on certainty; SUBJ if more doubtful If you follow Step 1 honestly—“am I asserting this is real?”—you’ll get most of those right.
Let’s talk about “quizás/tal vez.” I never know. I see both moods and it seems random.

It’s not random; it’s about degree of certainty and style. With quizá(s), tal vez, a lo mejor: - Subjunctive = more doubt/uncertainty, more “maybe” - Indicative = speaker leans toward it being true or treats it as plausible/likely Examples: - Quizás venga. (Maybe he’ll come.) → uncertain - Quizás viene. (Maybe he’s coming.) → speaker sounds more confident / contextual evidence Past: - Tal vez haya llegado. (Maybe he has arrived.) → uncertain about completion - Tal vez llegó. (Maybe he arrived.) → speaker suggests it likely happened In practice, many speakers choose subjunctive by default with these adverbs, especially when referring to future events. If you want a consistent rule: use subjunctive after quizás/tal vez unless you’re intentionally sounding more confident.
So I can just default to subjunctive with those and be safe?

Yes, that’s a good “safe default,” especially with future reference: - Quizás llueva mañana. - Tal vez venga más tarde. If you use indicative, you won’t necessarily be wrong, but it can subtly change the stance.
Another thing: “como si.” That’s always subjunctive, right? Like “como si fuera.”

Correct. “Como si” introduces an unreal comparison (“as if”), which uses subjunctive: - Habla como si supiera todo. (He talks as if he knew everything.) → imperfect subjunctive - Me miró como si hubiera visto un fantasma. (He looked at me as if he had seen a ghost.) → pluperfect subjunctive Even if the situation could be real, the structure treats it as hypothetical/contrary to the speaker’s assumption.
Imperfect subjunctive is another nightmare: -ra vs -se. Are they interchangeable? And when do I use imperfect subjunctive vs present subjunctive?

-ra and -se forms (hablara/hablase) are largely interchangeable in most contexts. Differences: - -ra is much more common in everyday speech and writing. - -se can sound more formal/literary or be used for stylistic variation. - In some fixed expressions or legal/formal texts, you might see more -se, but you can safely use -ra almost always. Present vs imperfect subjunctive depends on sequence of tenses and time reference: 1) Present subjunctive: when the main clause is present/future/imperative and the subordinate action is present/future relative to it: - Quiero que vengas. (now/future) - Es posible que llueva mañana. - Dudo que tenga tiempo. 2) Imperfect subjunctive: when the main clause is in a past tense (imperfect, preterite, conditional in some patterns) and the subordinate action is simultaneous or future-in-the-past: - Quería que vinieras. (I wanted you to come.) - Dudaba que tuviera tiempo. (I doubted he had time.) - Era posible que lloviera. (It was possible it would rain.) 3) Pluperfect subjunctive: when the subordinate action happened before the past reference point: - Dudaba que hubiera salido. (I doubted he had left.) And for conditionals: - Si tuviera…, (imperfect subj.) + …, iría (conditional) - Si hubiera tenido…, + …, habría ido (conditional perfect)
“Quería que vinieras” makes sense. But “Me dijo que viniera” is weird. Why not “vino” or “venías”? Also is that subjunctive or something else?

“Me dijo que viniera” is subjunctive (imperfect subjunctive), and it’s used because “decir” here is functioning as a verb of influence/command: “He told me to come.” Spanish distinguishes: - Decir that = reporting information → indicative: - Me dijo que venía. (He told me he was coming.) - Me dijo que vino. (He told me he came.) - Decir someone to do something (command/request) → subjunctive: - Me dijo que viniera. (He told me to come.) - Me dijo que no lo hiciera. (He told me not to do it.) So “me dijo que viniera” corresponds to “he told me to come,” not “he told me that he came.”
So “decir que” can be indicative or subjunctive depending on meaning. That’s like “me parece.” Great.

Yes, but the meaning difference is sharp and testable: - If you can paraphrase it as “he informed me that…” → indicative. - If you can paraphrase it as “he ordered/asked me to…” → subjunctive. Try these: 1) Me dijo que (estar) cansado. (He told me he was tired.) 2) Me dijo que (estar) más atento. (He told me to be more attentive.) You choose the forms.
1) estaba 2) estuviera
Correct: 1) estaba (indicative, reported state) 2) estuviera (subjunctive, instruction)
Let’s hit “cuando” again but with past. Like “Cuando llegó, lo vi” is indicative. But can you ever have “cuando llegara” in a time clause not conditional?

Yes. “Cuando” + imperfect subjunctive often appears in more literary/formal narration or when expressing a future-in-the-past (an event that was pending from a past viewpoint), or in certain set constructions. Examples: 1) Future-in-the-past / pending from past perspective: - Me prometió que me llamaría cuando llegara. (He promised he would call me when he arrived.) → from the promise time, arrival was future. 2) In reported speech with sequence of tenses: - Dijo que lo haría cuando pudiera. (He said he’d do it when he could.) 3) In conditional-like or hypothetical framing (not exactly “si,” but similar): - Cuando tuviera tiempo, lo haría. (When I had time, I would do it.) This can sound like a plan contingent on a condition; often you might also use “si.” But if you’re narrating a past event that actually happened: - Cuando llegó, lo vi. (indicative) So again: realized past event → indicative; pending/hypothetical from past standpoint → imperfect subjunctive.
That “pending from past viewpoint” is hard. Give me a pair.

Pair: A) Real, completed past: - Cuando terminó la reunión, me llamó. (He called me when the meeting ended.) → the meeting ended (fact). B) Future-in-the-past (reported plan): - Dijo que me llamaría cuando terminara la reunión. (He said he would call me when the meeting ended.) → at the time of saying, the end was future. The difference is whether the time clause is anchored to a real past event (A) or to a plan/promise in the past (B).
Okay. Another classic: “el hecho de que.” People say it triggers subjunctive, but I’ve also seen indicative. Which is it?

“El hecho de que…” is tricky because it can behave in two ways depending on whether it’s being presented as established fact or as something being evaluated/argued. Often, “el hecho de que” is used in argumentative contexts and tends to take subjunctive, especially when followed by an evaluation, reaction, or when it’s not treated as an accepted fact: - Me molesta el hecho de que no me llames. (subjunctive) → reaction But it can take indicative when it’s used to assert a fact (“the fact that…” as a factual premise): - El hecho de que no me llamas demuestra que no te importa. (indicative) → presenting as evidence, asserted fact. That said, many style guides note variation, and many speakers default to subjunctive after “el hecho de que.” If you want a consistent approach: - If the clause is treated as a premise/known fact → indicative. - If it’s treated as a situation being judged, doubted, or reacted to → subjunctive.
So it’s basically rhetorical stance again. Great. What about “porque”? I heard subjunctive can show “because” as alleged reason?

Yes, that’s a real and useful contrast. “Porque” normally takes indicative because reasons are presented as factual: - Lo hice porque tenía tiempo. (I did it because I had time.) → reason asserted. But subjunctive can appear when the reason is not asserted as true by the speaker—often in negative statements or when rejecting a presumed cause: - No lo hice porque tuviera tiempo. (I didn’t do it because I had time.) → meaning: not for that reason (maybe for another reason). The speaker is not affirming “I had time” as the cause; it’s being denied as the motivation. Compare: 1) No lo hice porque no tenía tiempo. (indicative) = I didn’t do it because I didn’t have time. (That was the reason.) 2) No lo hice porque tuviera tiempo. (subjunctive) = I didn’t do it because I had time. (That wasn’t the reason; maybe I had time, but that’s not why.) This is subtle but important.
That’s actually cool but also annoying. Give me more examples where subjunctive changes meaning like that.

Here are a few meaning-changing contrasts: 1) porque (reason asserted vs rejected) - No vino porque estaba enfermo. (He didn’t come because he was sick.) - No vino porque estuviera enfermo. (He didn’t come because he was sick [not that reason].) 2) aunque (fact vs hypothetical) - Aunque tiene dinero, no lo gasta. (Even though he has money…) - Aunque tenga dinero, no lo gastará. (Even if he has money…) 3) cuando (habitual vs future) - Cuando tengo tiempo, estudio. (Whenever I have time…) - Cuando tenga tiempo, estudiaré. (When I have time [later]…) 4) lo que (known vs desired in some structures) - Hago lo que puedo. (I do what I can.) → indicative (reality) - Haré lo que pueda. (I’ll do whatever I can.) → subjunctive (open-ended future) 5) quien (known vs unknown) - Hablo con quien me entiende. (I talk with whoever understands me [general/habitual], can be indicative) - Hablaré con quien me entienda. (I’ll talk with whoever understands me [in the future, whoever it may be]) → subjunctive These last ones (lo que/quien) follow the same “relative clause with non-specific antecedent” principle.
Wait, “Hago lo que puedo” vs “Haré lo que pueda” is a good one. Why does future automatically push subjunctive in those relative clauses?

Because in “Haré lo que pueda,” the “lo que” refers to an undefined set of actions that will be determined in the future; it’s not a fixed, known content right now. Spanish often uses subjunctive in relative clauses when the referent is indefinite or not yet identified—future contexts naturally create that. But it’s not “future automatically.” It’s “non-specific/not-yet-determined,” which often correlates with future. Compare: - Haré lo que puedo. (I will do what I can.) This can be okay if you mean your capacity is known/defined; but many speakers still prefer subjunctive because the actual actions are not known. “Haré lo que pueda” is the idiomatic choice.
Another one I see: “Sea lo que sea.” What is that? Subjunctive of ser, but why?

“Sea lo que sea” literally is “be it what it may,” using subjunctive to express indifference/unknown identity: - Sea lo que sea, no voy a cambiar de opinión. (Whatever it is, I’m not going to change my mind.) It’s a fixed concessive/indefinite expression. Similar patterns: - Pase lo que pase. (Whatever happens.) - Diga lo que diga. (Whatever he says.) - Hagas lo que hagas. (Whatever you do.) All subjunctive because the content is unknown/irrelevant to the main clause.
Okay, give me a training plan. Not motivational stuff, an actual plan: what to drill, in what order, with what kind of sentences. I want to stop wasting time.

Here’s a concrete, technical plan (4 weeks, but you can compress/expand). The key is isolating variables. Week 1: Noun clauses (QUE-clauses) — high frequency triggers Goal: instant recognition of triggers; present subjunctive forms. Drills: - 20 sentences/day where you only label IND vs SUBJ (no conjugation). - 20 sentences/day conjugation with a small verb set: ser/estar/tener/hacer/ir/poder/venir/decir. Focus sets: A) Fact/assertion: saber que, es cierto que, es evidente que, ver que B) Doubt/denial: dudar que, no creer que, negar que, no es cierto que C) Desire/influence: querer que, pedir que, recomendar que, prohibir que D) Emotion/judgment: alegrarse de que, molestar que, es importante que Week 2: Time/purpose/condition adverbial clauses Goal: automatic subjunctive in future time clauses; “para que” and “hasta que.” Drills: - Transformations: rewrite indicative habitual → future plan with subjunctive. - Cuando tengo tiempo, estudio → Cuando tenga tiempo, estudiaré. - Mix “antes de que / después de que / en cuanto / hasta que / para que.” Week 3: Relative clauses (adjective clauses) Goal: specific vs non-specific; negation effects. Drills: - Pairs: “Busco un X que…” vs “Conozco el X que…” - Negation: “Hay alguien que…” vs “No hay nadie que…” Week 4: Meaning contrasts + mixed review Goal: interpret stance shifts (aunque, porque, quizá). Drills: - Choose mood AND explain meaning difference in one sentence. - Short paragraph production: 6–8 sentences using at least 6 subjunctive triggers. If you want, I can generate daily drill sets and correct them quickly.
Give me a daily drill set right now. Like 40 items. But make them hard and varied, not the same “quiero que” over and over.

Here are 40 items. For each, choose the correct mood and conjugate the verb in parentheses. Mix of present subjunctive, imperfect subjunctive, indicative, and a couple perfect forms. 1) No niego que él (tener) talento. 2) Niego que él (tener) talento. 3) Me sorprende que ustedes no (haber) llamado. 4) Es evidente que ustedes no (haber) llamado. 5) Buscamos un programa que (funcionar) sin errores. 6) Tenemos el programa que (funcionar) sin errores. 7) No hay ningún estudiante que (saber) esa regla. 8) Hay un estudiante que (saber) esa regla. 9) Te ayudaré cuando (terminar) mi trabajo. 10) Siempre me llama cuando (terminar) su trabajo. 11) Me pidió que no le (decir) nada a nadie. 12) Me dijo que no le (decir) nada a nadie. (He told me that he didn’t tell anyone.) 13) Me dijo que no le (decir) nada a nadie. (He told me not to tell anyone.) 14) Aunque (ser) difícil, lo vamos a intentar. (It is difficult.) 15) Aunque (ser) difícil, lo vamos a intentar. (Even if it’s difficult.) 16) No lo hago porque (querer) quedar bien. 17) No lo hago porque no (querer) quedar bien. 18) Quizás (venir) mañana, pero no estoy seguro. 19) Tal vez (estar) en casa; las luces están encendidas. 20) Es posible que (haber) un error. 21) Es cierto que (haber) un error. 22) Insistieron en que nosotros (participar). 23) Era mejor que nosotros (participar). 24) Es mejor que nosotros (participar). 25) Si yo (tener) más tiempo, lo haría. 26) Si yo (haber) tenido más tiempo, lo habría hecho. 27) Me alegra que me lo (decir). 28) Me alegra saber que me lo (decir). 29) No creo que (ser) necesario. 30) Creo que no (ser) necesario. 31) No conozco a nadie que (poder) hacerlo. 32) Conozco a alguien que (poder) hacerlo. 33) Esperaba que tú (venir) temprano. (past) 34) Espero que tú (venir) temprano. (present) 35) Esperé a que tú (venir). (waited) 36) Saldré en cuanto (acabar) esto. 37) Salí en cuanto (acabar) esto. 38) El hecho de que ella no me (responder) me preocupa. 39) El hecho de que ella no me (responder) demuestra algo. 40) Sea lo que (ser), no pienso discutir.
